title: Carded and Discarded

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso does a whiny impression of himself not wanting to go to work, then earnestly declares he says 'Hot dog, let me at 'em!'
jokes.setup: Rosso is framing his job with inspirational positivity, doing a self-deprecating whine impression
jokes.punchline: 'Hot dog, let me at 'em!' — the phrase is so corny and earnest it undercuts any wisdom he's trying to convey
jokes.explanation: The 'hot dog' phrase is perfectly calibrated to Rosso's oblivious enthusiasm — it's not just a random silly phrase, it's exactly the kind of thing this character would say with complete sincerity. The humor comes from how earnestly he owns it.

jokes.text: Student flatly asks: 'Do you actually say the words Hot Dog?'
jokes.setup: Rosso has just delivered 'Hot dog, let me at 'em!' as inspirational wisdom
jokes.punchline: The student's deadpan clarifying question implies total disbelief that any adult human being uses this phrase
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — five words of deadpan that land because the student is speaking for the entire audience. The straight-man delivery is the joke; no embellishment needed.

jokes.text: Rosso references Alice Cooper as someone even cool kids respect, then begins performing 'I'm 18' on guitar for a room of students
jokes.setup: Rosso cites Alice Cooper as proof that adults can understand teen angst
jokes.punchline: He actually picks up a guitar and starts performing the song himself — the cringe of an authority figure trying desperately to connect through rock music
jokes.explanation: The sustained cringe of a guidance counselor performing Alice Cooper for captive students is well-executed. The joke builds across the performance — it's not one moment but an escalating discomfort. Character-specific: only Rosso would think this is helping.

jokes.text: Rosso asks the students mid-song 'Come on Nick, fill in those drums there, will ya?' expecting participation
jokes.setup: Rosso is already performing Alice Cooper in a guidance session
jokes.punchline: He now solicits student drum accompaniment, fully committed to turning this into a jam session
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the bit — from 'I understand you' to 'fill in those drums' — deepens the cringe. The fact that he knows Nick plays drums adds a layer of sad specificity.

jokes.text: 'Oh my God. That was brutal. I've never hated Alice Cooper as much as I do right now.'
jokes.setup: Rosso has just finished his earnest Alice Cooper performance
jokes.punchline: Kim's response is not just criticism of Rosso but inadvertently redirects blame to Alice Cooper himself — a perfectly illogical but emotionally true reaction
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. The logic is absurdist but emotionally precise — of course you'd blame Alice Cooper for this. Kim's deadpan delivery and the complete lack of sympathy for Rosso makes it land hard. Highly quotable, character-specific (only Kim would phrase it this way), and economical. Approaches an 8 on craft — it's sharp, original, and perfectly structured.

jokes.text: Lindsay defends Rosso: 'Guys, Mr. Rosso's really good at the guitar. Some of those chords are hard.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is reacting with horror to Rosso's performance
jokes.punchline: Lindsay earnestly defends him on technical merits, missing the social point entirely — revealing her own awkward in-between status
jokes.explanation: Good character work — Lindsay is still enough of a good-girl goody-two-shoes that she instinctively defends the teacher while the freaks groan. The joke is in what it reveals about her not-quite-belonging.

jokes.text: Weir counselor uses Einstein and Thomas Edison as examples of people who didn't go to college — then adds 'Frank. The dude who pumps my gas.'
jokes.setup: Rosso is trying to inspire Lindsay by citing examples of successful non-college-goers — Einstein, Edison...
jokes.punchline: 'Frank. The dude who pumps my gas.' — the list peaks with genuinely great historical figures then plummets to a random local man Rosso knows
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The structure is immaculate: establish a pattern of genuine greatness (Einstein, Edison), make the audience anticipate a third great figure, then deliver Frank the gas station attendant with the same gravity. The pause before 'Frank. Frank who?' then 'The dude who pumps my gas' is perfect comic construction. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific (Rosso genuinely thinks Frank is worth citing), and the economy is flawless. Justifiably above 8.

jokes.text: Harold's wife mentions the birthday card came with $300 through the mail. Harold immediately worries: 'She's lucky that wasn't intercepted by some junkie working for the post office.'
jokes.setup: Family receives news of a $300 birthday gift mailed in a card
jokes.punchline: Harold immediately catastrophizes about postal junkies — a very specific paranoid worldview delivered as though it's common sense
jokes.explanation: Harold's paranoid dad energy is well-deployed here. The specificity of 'junkie working for the post office' is the key — it's not just 'someone could steal it' but a very vivid, slightly uncharitable mental image that tells us everything about Harold.

jokes.text: Harold excitedly describes the family game Pit: 'We all yell across the table at each other trying to get the other players to trade stocks with us.' Lindsay responds: 'So we all sit around the table, and you yell at us? You do that to us every night.'
jokes.setup: Harold pitches Pit as a fun family bonding game — describing the mechanic of yelling across the table
jokes.punchline: Lindsay points out this is indistinguishable from their normal family dynamic
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke — the setup is inadvertently self-describing and Lindsay's retort makes the implicit explicit. It's also character work: Lindsay is sharp enough to land this, and Harold's earnestness makes the setup land. Clean, well-earned.

jokes.text: Harold's passionate parental speech escalates to: 'When you two were kids, you used to run around naked and lay in bed with us all night. We bathed you, and we cleaned your butts when you pooped. And we loved it.'
jokes.setup: Harold is giving an emotional speech about family closeness and feeling disrespected
jokes.punchline: His evidence of their bond is graphic bathroom-related baby memories, delivered with complete earnestness — the humor is in what he thinks is a persuasive argument
jokes.explanation: The cringe is earned — Harold keeps escalating his argument in a direction that makes it worse for himself while thinking he's making it better. 'And we loved it' is the kicker that transforms discomfort into comedy.

jokes.text: Harold concludes his emotional family speech with: 'We are going to spend quality time together, and we're going to enjoy it, damn it!'
jokes.setup: Harold has been pleading for the family to want to be together
jokes.punchline: He commands them to enjoy themselves — the contradiction of ordering people to have fun undercuts his entire emotional appeal
jokes.explanation: The logic of forced fun is a solid comedic concept and Harold's total lack of self-awareness about the contradiction makes it land. The 'damn it' is the right punctuation — he's committed to warmth through fury.

jokes.text: Biology teacher reacts to knock on door during his lecture on natural selection: 'Well, that might be Mr. Darwin now.'
jokes.setup: Teacher is mid-lecture about natural selection/Darwin when there's a knock at the door
jokes.punchline: The dry suggestion that Darwin himself might be visiting — perfectly timed to his own subject matter
jokes.explanation: A functional teacher-joke — the timing is good and it's delivered with appropriate dryness. Not particularly original as a type of joke but executed economically.

jokes.text: Biology teacher tells the new transfer student: 'Always room for another head on the chopping block.'
jokes.setup: Teacher is welcoming a new student to class
jokes.punchline: His welcome uses a grimly inappropriate metaphor — 'chopping block' — as though it's a normal thing to say to a teenager
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy economy — the teacher doesn't register that 'chopping block' is an alarming phrase. Sets up this teacher as a specific type of oblivious/dark personality, which pays off later.

jokes.text: Neal opens the conversation with the new girl: 'I saw Ronald Reagan kissing a monkey on TV last night.' New girl asks 'On the news?' Neal: 'No, no, no. It was in a movie. He's a pretty good actor.' Beat. 'So was the monkey.'
jokes.setup: Neal opens with the random Reagan-monkey observation; Maureen asks if it was news (reasonable concern given Reagan is president); Neal clarifies it was a movie
jokes.punchline: 'So was the monkey' — after granting Reagan some compliment, Neal delivers the comedic pivot that undercuts it, while 'so was the monkey' also lands as a political observation
jokes.explanation: Excellent construction. The Maureen 'on the news?' line gets a laugh on its own (because it's 1980 and Reagan is president, so monkey-kissing on the news is plausible), then the punchline 'so was the monkey' works on two levels — pure comedy and light political commentary. Economy is near-perfect. One of the episode's stronger joke structures.

jokes.text: Sam tells Maureen 'My dad drives a Coup de Ville, so we're practically related' after she says her dad works for General Motors
jokes.setup: Maureen says her dad was transferred to work for General Motors
jokes.punchline: Sam's leap in logic — owning a GM car = practically family — is the kind of nerdy social-desperation non-sequitur that reveals his character
jokes.explanation: Character comedy gold — this is exactly how a socially desperate teenage boy would try to create connection. It's awkward without being painful, and very Sam-specific.

jokes.text: The geeks bond with Maureen through increasingly grim teacher-injury stories: teacher ran over old lady and blamed his accelerator, another teacher shot a burglar, shop teacher got scalp torn off by a lathe, shop teacher's pinky cut off by radial arm saw and 'now everyone calls him Pinky Tuskadero'
jokes.setup: Geeks trying to make a good impression on the new girl by sharing school stories
jokes.punchline: Each story tops the last in violence/injury; the sequence culminates in 'Pinky Tuskadero' — a Happy Days reference applied to a maimed shop teacher — and Maureen's genuine delight
jokes.explanation: The escalating injury parade is well-structured dark comedy, and 'Pinky Tuskadero' is a perfect punchline — a Happy Days reference applied to real dismemberment, landing the absurdist dark comedy note cleanly. The payoff ('I'm really glad I sat here') makes it emotionally coherent too.

jokes.text: 'But if I start bugging you, please be sure and let me know.' / 'Oh, don't worry. We'd never let you bug us.' — The geeks' inner monologue reaction to this is clearly visible (they would never say that).
jokes.setup: Maureen humbly asks them to tell her if she's being a burden
jokes.punchline: The boys say they'd never let her bug them — ironic because they are DESPERATELY hoping she'll stay and wouldn't dream of pushing her away; the gap between their answer and their interior state is the joke
jokes.explanation: Mild irony — the joke is in the subtext. Works within the naturalistic tone of the show but doesn't have a strong punchline, more a gentle ironic beat.

jokes.text: Nick declares 'Everything fun in this world happens in bars.'
jokes.setup: The freaks are lamenting they can't get into bars
jokes.punchline: Nick's sweeping philosophical declaration is delivered with total conviction — its grandiosity relative to the mundane subject is the joke
jokes.explanation: A functional character beat — Nick's earnest worldview is mildly funny. Not a standout but fits the naturalistic rhythm of the scene.

jokes.text: Kim shows her fake I.D. — her cousin's license. The geeks react: 'Oh my God, this looks exactly like you.' Kim: 'I know.' / 'Except it says you're 24.' Kim: 'So? I look mature. Nobody ever stops me.'
jokes.setup: Kim produces a fake I.D. — her cousin's old license — and they marvel at the resemblance
jokes.punchline: Kim's completely straight-faced confidence that looking 24 at whatever age she is is natural and unremarkable is the joke — she's fully committed to the bit
jokes.explanation: Kim's self-confidence is the joke — she genuinely sees no problem with this. Character-specific and delivered with the right deadpan.

jokes.text: When asked if Daniel has a fake I.D., everyone laughs, then Daniel explains: 'Some stupid bouncer asked what my star sign was. I didn't know, so he snagged it.'
jokes.setup: The group laughs knowingly at whether Daniel has a fake I.D.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's story of losing it to the most basic of bouncers — the star sign question — reveals both his incompetence and his indignation
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy. Daniel is cool enough to have a fake I.D. but not smart enough to know his fake birthday's star sign — a very specific, believable failure that says everything about him.

jokes.text: Nick tries to help Daniel with star signs: 'Capricorn! Cancer! Capricorn! Oh!' Daniel: 'Dude, I hate astrology. What, everybody born in the same month is gonna have the same life?'
jokes.setup: Nick is shouting star sign corrections at Daniel after the fact
jokes.punchline: Daniel's dismissal of astrology as a concept — while his fake I.D. was confiscated specifically because he didn't know his (fake) star sign — is perfectly ironic
jokes.explanation: The irony is well-constructed — Daniel dismisses astrology precisely because he was caught by it. His 'what, everybody born in the same month is gonna have the same life?' is a genuine attempt at logic that only highlights his own failure.

jokes.text: After Lindsay offers to lend money for fake I.D.s, Daniel says: 'Well, well, well. I don't know why, but I'm liking her more and more every day.' Ken: 'Join the club.' Lindsay: 'Ew.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay offers to fund fake I.D.s for the group
jokes.punchline: Ken's 'Join the club' — delivered as though there's a formal organization — then Lindsay's 'Ew' which shuts down whatever he was implying
jokes.explanation: Efficient three-beat joke. 'Join the club' is the strongest element — implies Daniel's feelings for Lindsay are already a group phenomenon. Lindsay's 'Ew' is the right button-press.

jokes.text: Harris pitches the new girl on the A.V. room's Beta machine. The geeks quickly shut him down: 'Thank you, Harris. We're very late, though.'
jokes.setup: Harris tries to impress Maureen with the A.V. room's new equipment
jokes.punchline: The geeks immediately and firmly shut him down — a visual/behavioral moment where we see them protectively cutting off the competition while maintaining polite cover
jokes.explanation: Mild territorial comedy — the geeks immediately recognizing and neutralizing the rival is funny but not exceptional. Works as naturalistic character behavior.

jokes.text: Gordon tells Maureen: 'Tomorrow's pizza day in the cafeteria. It's made out of real tomato sauce and everything.'
jokes.setup: Gordon tries to impress the new girl with school amenities
jokes.punchline: 'Real tomato sauce' as a selling point — Gordon's enthusiasm for the lowest possible bar is both sad and funny
jokes.explanation: Sweet and small. The 'real tomato sauce and everything' qualifier does the work — Gordon's standards are endearingly low and his pride in the fact is very in-character.

jokes.text: Bill tells Maureen he doesn't have to be home until dinner. Gordon says: 'I don't have to be home ever.'
jokes.setup: Making casual small talk about after-school plans
jokes.punchline: Gordon's 'I don't have to be home ever' — delivered flatly, implying a level of parental indifference that's either sad or liberating (or both)
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect economy — five words that imply an entire family situation while landing as dry comedy. The contrast with Bill's normal 'not until dinner' is the setup-punchline structure in miniature.

jokes.text: At the clothing store, Lindsay announces 'We need some fake I.D.s' loudly. Howie frantically shushes her then corrects: 'I mean, we need some slacks.'
jokes.setup: They've come to Howie's workplace to buy fake I.D.s discreetly
jokes.punchline: Lindsay announces it at full volume; Howie's desperate cover story ('slacks') is both inadequate and perfectly mundane
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat — the immediate social disaster and desperate cover is well-executed. 'Slacks' as the cover word is a nice choice: exactly the kind of panicked, age-inappropriate word a nervous teenager would blurt.

jokes.text: Howie shows off 'genuine Canadian drivers' licenses' as though Canadian provenance is a mark of quality
jokes.setup: Howie promises to find matches from a shipment of licenses
jokes.punchline: 'Genuine Canadian drivers' licenses' — the word 'genuine' is funny applied to fake I.D.s, and the Canadian origin is presented as a feature rather than a flaw
jokes.explanation: A functional absurdist joke — 'genuine' fake I.D.s from Canada is a pleasant contradiction. Howie's salesmanship is the character note that makes it land.

jokes.text: Howie slowly memorizes each person's face while repeating 'Brown eyes, brown hair' for each one identically
jokes.setup: Howie claims he needs to 'memorize faces' to find matching Canadian ID photos
jokes.punchline: Every person gets the exact same assessment — 'brown eyes, brown hair' — implying he has no actual differentiation system and/or all Canadians look the same to him
jokes.explanation: The running repetition is the structure of the joke — three times 'brown eyes, brown hair' for visually different people. It perfectly reveals the scam's mechanism: Howie doesn't actually match anyone, he just assigns whatever he has. The solemnity of his inspection makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Howie's boss interrupts: 'There's a box of Parisian Nightsuits in the back, and they're not gonna unpack themselves.' Howie covers: 'I'm just helping these customers.' Then to the group: 'These look great, sir. These look great.'
jokes.setup: Howie is conducting illegal fake I.D. business on work time; his boss arrives
jokes.punchline: Howie's simultaneous cover to his boss and assurance to the kids — 'these look great, sir, these look great' — is chaotically trying to please everyone at once
jokes.explanation: Standard workplace-crime-cover cringe but executed with enough specificity (Parisian Nightsuits!) to earn its place.

jokes.text: Ken says 'Had to bring the big rocket, didn't you?' then Bill responds: 'What? So I got a big rocket. What am I supposed to do, cut it in half?'
jokes.setup: They're about to launch model rockets with Maureen
jokes.punchline: Ken's faux-jealous tone about the rocket size plays as innuendo; Bill's earnest defensive reply ('cut it in half') is funny because he's completely literal while the double meaning hangs in the air
jokes.explanation: Mild double entendre. The joke is partly in Bill's complete obliviousness and Ken's faint awareness. Works for the age-appropriate humor of the scene.

jokes.text: Neal fantasizes aloud: 'What if I'm asleep right now and this is just a dream, and any moment Maureen and I are gonna make love?' Sam: 'Ew.' Neal: 'Then what are we doing here? Maybe this is my nightmare.'
jokes.setup: Neal is overcome with joy that Maureen is hanging out with them
jokes.punchline: He voices his fantasy, gets 'Ew' from Sam, then re-frames Sam's presence as the nightmare version of the dream — good pivot
jokes.explanation: Good three-beat structure. The 'make love' phrasing is slightly old-fashioned and awkward in the right Neal-specific way. The nightmare re-frame is the best part — using Sam's 'Ew' as setup for a second joke is efficient.

jokes.text: Maureen sees Bill's rocket: 'God, Bill, your rocket's huge.' Bill: 'Oh, it is? I hadn't noticed.'
jokes.setup: Callback to the rocket size joke — Maureen now inadvertently delivers the same innuendo
jokes.punchline: Bill's 'Oh, it is? I hadn't noticed' — completely guileless, which makes it funnier than a knowing response would be
jokes.explanation: The callback works because Bill's innocence is the consistent joke. The double-meaning is available for those who want it, but Bill operates on a purely literal level throughout.

jokes.text: Discussion of whether you could be with someone you couldn't fart in front of. Bill: 'She's the kind of woman you could cut the cheese in front of.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are philosophizing about what a relationship with Maureen could be like
jokes.punchline: Bill's romantic ideal distilled to flatulence tolerance — 'cut the cheese' as relationship criterion — is both absurd and somehow touching
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in-character for Bill — his romantic worldview is filtered entirely through physical comfort. The 'cut the cheese' phrasing rather than more direct language is exactly right for his character and era.

jokes.text: Bill extends the flatulence-in-love philosophy: 'You have to sleep with your wife every night of your life. If you couldn't blast one in bed, you'd get physically ill.'
jokes.setup: Neal pushes back on Bill's cheese-cutting theory
jokes.punchline: Bill escalates his argument to medical necessity — 'you'd get physically ill' — treating this as a serious physiological fact, with total earnestness
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Bill's fart-philosophy to medical necessity is the kind of committed absurdist logic that Freaks and Geeks does brilliantly. Bill is completely serious, which makes it funnier. Character-specific: only Bill would develop this argument this earnestly.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Oh, come on, guys. You can't fart in front of a woman.' Bill: 'What do you think you do, get up and run outside every time you have to let one rip? You'd never get any sleep.'
jokes.setup: Neal challenges Bill's fart-in-front-of-partner theory
jokes.punchline: Bill's reductio ad absurdum — 'you'd never get any sleep' — treats the outdoor fart scenario as a serious logistical problem requiring formal solution
jokes.explanation: Part of a well-sustained bit. Bill's logical consistency within his absurd framework is the joke — he's thought this through, and his conclusion ('you'd never get any sleep') is, within his logic, completely reasonable.

jokes.text: Sam: 'I think I might like Maureen, too.' Neal: 'Wait. I thought you liked Cindy Sanders.' Sam: 'Well, I mean, I do. But Maureen talks to us. She's not as scary.'
jokes.setup: Sam admits he may also have feelings for Maureen
jokes.punchline: 'She's not as scary' — Sam's romantic criteria is literally 'will acknowledge my existence' — a crushingly honest self-assessment of his social standing
jokes.explanation: Very character-specific and emotionally true. 'She's not as scary' is both funny and a little heartbreaking — it's an honest articulation of what social accessibility means when you're at the bottom of the hierarchy. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Neal: 'She clearly likes me best.' Sam: 'How do you know?' Neal: 'You see the way she looks at me? She can barely contain herself.' Sam: 'You just happen to be looking at her every time she's thinking about me.'
jokes.setup: Neal claims Maureen's looks prove she likes him best
jokes.punchline: Sam's counter-logic — 'you just happen to be looking at her every time she's thinking about me' — is equally delusional and perfectly mirrors Neal's reasoning
jokes.explanation: Sam's retort is structurally clever — he uses Neal's exact (flawed) logic against him with equal confidence. The mutual delusion is funny and true to both characters. 'She can barely contain herself' from Neal is also a great line.

jokes.text: They decide who gets to ask Maureen out by the same method they used to decide who stood in line for Empire Strikes Back tickets — drawing from a hat.
jokes.setup: They need a fair method to determine who gets to pursue Maureen
jokes.punchline: The Empire Strikes Back lottery precedent — that they've formalized this kind of decision before, and for an equally momentous occasion (in their minds), is perfectly in-character
jokes.explanation: Excellent character integration — the geeks have a precedent for this kind of lottery, and Empire Strikes Back is exactly the level of stakes they'd apply the same methodology to. Tells us everything about how their friendship works.

jokes.text: Jean tells Harold: 'You cannot force these kids to spend time with us.' Harold: 'Did you know that in some cultures, if your children shame you, you're allowed to have them executed?'
jokes.setup: Jean is moderating Harold's frustration with the kids' reluctance to do family night
jokes.punchline: Harold's sudden pivot to execution as a parenting alternative — presented with the tone of a man genuinely considering it — is the best Harold joke in the episode
jokes.explanation: This is Harold at his comedic peak. The escalation from parental frustration to 'have them executed' is surprising, specific ('some cultures' signals he's thought about this), and delivered with just enough plausibility to be alarming. The follow-up 'I'm not swinging an ax. I just want them to play Pit with me' is what makes it great — the self-moderation implies he considered the ax option. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Jean offers the 'if you love something, set it free' wisdom. Harold grudgingly agrees: 'All right. There you go. They're free. Let 'em free. Let 'em go.' Immediately turns: 'Get back here.'
jokes.setup: Jean gives Harold the 'set it free' philosophy as parenting wisdom and he performatively accepts it
jokes.punchline: 'Get back here' — Harold's embrace of the philosophy lasts approximately two seconds before his instinct takes over
jokes.explanation: The comedic timing of this beat is excellent — Harold's capitulation is almost sincere before he immediately reverses. 'Get back here' is three words doing a lot of work. The speed of the reversal is the whole joke.

jokes.text: Bill wins the Maureen lottery. Neal and Sam's visible disappointment vs. Bill's 'I'm the happiest man in the world.'
jokes.setup: Bill draws the winning paper from the hat
jokes.punchline: Bill's complete transformation into the 'happiest man in the world' — a phrase disproportionate to the situation — and the immediate misery of the other two
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat — 'happiest man in the world' is disproportionate and Bill's earnestness makes it land. The contrast with the others' misery works visually.

jokes.text: Sam accuses Bill of cheating: 'He wet his paper with spit. That's how he wins every time. He just feels for the wet one.'
jokes.setup: Bill wins the lottery; Sam immediately cries foul
jokes.punchline: The existence of a sophisticated spit-based cheating methodology — and the fact that 'he wins every time' implies this is an established pattern — implies these boys have done this lottery many times
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Bill has a practiced spit-cheating technique is both absurd and character-specific — it deepens the worldbuilding around these three having a formal lottery system with an established winner. The detail that 'he wins every time' is the richest part.

jokes.text: Bill announces: 'Mrs. Maureen Haverchuck. Has a nice ring to it, don't you think?'
jokes.setup: Bill has just won the right to pursue Maureen
jokes.punchline: Bill is already trying out married names — having won a piece of paper, he's planning the wedding
jokes.explanation: Bill's leap from 'won a lottery' to 'trying out married names' is the perfect escalation of his earnest romanticism. 'Has a nice ring to it, don't you think?' as he consults the losers is an extra beat of obliviousness.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Great, Bill. Now you can go and fart in front of her. You'll make quite the impression.'
jokes.setup: Bill has won the right to court Maureen
jokes.punchline: Neal's sarcasm references Bill's earlier fart-philosophy — the callback lands because Bill's romantic theory is now his only known quality as a suitor
jokes.explanation: The callback to the fart philosophy pays off well — Neal's sarcasm is sharper for the reference. 'You'll make quite the impression' is the right amount of acid. Good callback structure.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso offers Lindsay Ivy League brochures: 'Several are in color.' Lindsay: 'Mr. Rosso, why can't you just leave me alone?' Rosso: 'I heard the words, but your eyes said something else. They said, I'm lost. I need your help. Don't listen to me. Guide me.'
jokes.setup: Rosso intercepts Lindsay with college brochures
jokes.punchline: Rosso completely inverts what Lindsay's eyes are saying — attributing to her a plea he clearly wants to hear, with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: Strong Rosso character comedy. 'Several are in color' as a selling point for Ivy League brochures is a perfect small absurdity, but the main joke — Rosso completely reinterpreting Lindsay's rejection as a secret plea — is the real engine here. Highly character-specific: Rosso hears what he needs to hear.

jokes.text: Rosso's threatened alternative: 'Tell you what, why don't I just come and visit you in the prison where you'll be living, and give you some really good advice, like should you get shanked in the yard or the dining hall? When you have your baby, which prison guard should take care of it?'
jokes.setup: Rosso is sarcastically offering to help Lindsay if she won't listen to his college advice
jokes.punchline: His prison scenario escalates to include shanking locations and prison guard childcare — the specificity of the prison fantasy is simultaneously alarming and absurd
jokes.explanation: Rosso's dark imagination about Lindsay's future is extended but earns its length through escalation — each new prison detail is weirder than the last. 'Which prison guard should take care of it' is an inspired final absurdity. Character-specific: only Rosso would frame concern this way.

jokes.text: Bill announces he's ready to talk to Maureen: 'Never been readier.' Then: 'I'm just gonna let the love...flow.'
jokes.setup: The boys are prepping Bill for his romantic approach
jokes.punchline: 'Let the love...flow' — the pause before 'flow' suggests even Bill can't quite commit to the phrase, but he says it anyway
jokes.explanation: The hesitation before 'flow' does a lot of comedic work — it's Bill sensing the phrase is perhaps too much, then deciding it's exactly right. Subtle character timing.

jokes.text: As soon as it's time for Bill to talk to Maureen, he panics: 'I gotta get something out of my locker.' The other two: 'What are you doing? Go on.'
jokes.setup: Bill was confident he was ready; Maureen approaches
jokes.punchline: He immediately abandons his plan and retreats to the locker — the classic confidence-to-flight reversal
jokes.explanation: A reliable cringe beat — the gap between stated readiness and actual behavior. Not exceptional but works in context.

jokes.text: Maureen starts talking to Vicki the cheerleader. Sam's group panics: 'The minute she starts getting with that crowd, we'll never see her again.' 'Because they're pod people. It's like Invasion of the Body Snatchers. They'll make her one of them.' 'We'll see her in a week, and she'll be all...' [SCREECHING]
jokes.setup: Maureen talks to a cheerleader; the geeks panic
jokes.punchline: The Invasion of the Body Snatchers analysis of cheerleader social dynamics, culminating in an actual alien-pod-person screech impression
jokes.explanation: The Body Snatchers reference is perfect for these characters — they communicate social anxiety through sci-fi/genre film. The screech impression is the physical comedy button that elevates it from clever observation to actual comedy.

jokes.text: The geeks' emergency plan to extract Maureen from the cheerleaders: deploying Eli by telling him Vicki said Three's Company isn't funny. Eli immediately storms over.
jokes.setup: The geeks need to interrupt Maureen's conversation with Vicki; they identify Eli as their tool
jokes.punchline: They weaponize Eli's Three's Company devotion — and it works instantly, perfectly — he goes off like a bomb
jokes.explanation: Wonderful character-based absurdist comedy. The geeks have identified Eli's specific and intense passion, constructed a lie precisely calibrated to trigger him, and deployed him like a human missile. The fact that it works perfectly — Eli immediately abandons all social context — is the payoff. Highly character-specific and structurally satisfying.

jokes.text: Eli launches into a Three's Company defense to Vicki: 'The Ropers don't want Jack living with two girls because he doesn't want any dirty stuff going on in his apartment building. There was this scene where Jack had lost his voice...'
jokes.setup: Eli has been deployed to interrupt Maureen's conversation by confronting Vicki about Three's Company
jokes.punchline: Eli's explanation of Three's Company's premise — completely earnest, completely oblivious to the social context — captures Eli as a character while letting the geeks escape with Maureen
jokes.explanation: The callback delivers on the setup — Eli's passionate explanation of the Roper's landlord-based concerns is exactly as awkward as expected. Character-specific and part of a well-structured comedic operation.

jokes.text: Howie hands out the fake Canadian I.D.s: 'Who the hell is Jesus H. Garcia?' / 'It's Hey-Soos. It's your new identity.'
jokes.setup: Howie distributes the I.D.s; the recipients examine their new identities
jokes.punchline: Nick's confusion about 'Jesus H. Garcia' and Howie's correction of the pronunciation — plus the confidence that this Hispanic identity for a clearly non-Hispanic person will work
jokes.explanation: The pronunciation correction is a good beat — Howie's insistence on 'Hey-Soos' implies he considers this authentic, which is itself the absurdity. The series of identity mismatches that follow build on this well.

jokes.text: The geeks' fake I.D. photos 'look kinda Chinese.' Howie's explanation: 'There's a big Vietnamese community up there in Alberta.'
jokes.setup: The I.D.s clearly don't match the recipients' appearances — multiple people note they look East Asian
jokes.punchline: Howie's explanation — a large Vietnamese community in Alberta — presented as though this fully explains and resolves the situation
jokes.explanation: Howie's explanation is a masterwork of confident nonsense — the Alberta Vietnamese community is a genuine fact deployed as though it obviates the obvious problem. The delivery must be total confidence for this to work, and the absurdity of the logic is the joke. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'This says I'm 29.' Howie: 'That doesn't matter. Mine says I'm 34. I have no problems. It's fine.'
jokes.setup: Daniel objects to being identified as 29 on his fake I.D.
jokes.punchline: Howie's 34 as a trump card — as though being assigned a higher age than Daniel makes Howie's situation better, not worse
jokes.explanation: Howie's logic is inverted — he presents 34 as evidence that 29 is fine, when 34 is clearly a bigger problem. His 'I have no problems' while possessing an I.D. saying he's 34 is confident absurdist comedy.

jokes.text: Howie defends the I.D. quality when asked for a refund: 'It's hard to find matches for you guys. No one in Canada looks like you.'
jokes.setup: The group demands their money back after seeing the mismatched I.D.s
jokes.punchline: Howie blames Canada — not his own limitations — for the mismatch. The implication that the failure is geographic is a perfect con-artist deflection
jokes.explanation: Perfectly delivered con-man logic. Howie shifting blame to Canadian demographics is funny and character-specific — he's incapable of admitting fault and finds a geographical scapegoat. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Howie's parting gift/consolation: 'Oh, by the way, tomorrow at Silverman's we're having a big sale — half off on all dress shirts. Maybe you guys come by, I'll hook you up.'
jokes.setup: The group has just learned their I.D.s are unusable; Howie is concluding business
jokes.punchline: Howie's pivot to clothing sale promotion — treating it as a reasonable consolation for terrible fake I.D.s — is the perfect coda to his character
jokes.explanation: Howie pivoting to his legitimate business after conducting an illegitimate one is a beautiful character button. The 'I'll hook you up' on dress shirts after failing them completely is tone-deaf in exactly the right way.

jokes.text: Bill sidles up to Maureen and immediately talks about pudding skin: 'I like pudding, too. Yeah, I like it when it's been sitting around for a while and it gets that skin on the top.'
jokes.setup: Bill has won the right to court Maureen; he finally gets a moment with her
jokes.punchline: His romantic opening is an unsolicited enthusiasm for pudding skin — delivered with the same earnestness as his fart philosophy
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest character jokes. Bill's romantic strategy — pudding skin — is so specifically, perfectly wrong that it becomes a kind of genius. Only Bill would open a romantic pursuit with this. The commitment is total, the subject is unexpected, and it tells us everything about him. High character integration because only Bill Haverchuck opens this way.

jokes.text: Maureen mentions she's been invited to Vicki's party. Bill's visible reaction — a smile collapsing, a brave attempt to seem happy for her — inferred from context of others' awkward silence and 'Wasn't that nice of her?'
jokes.setup: Bill is pursuing Maureen; she casually mentions the popular-kids' party invitation
jokes.punchline: The 'Wasn't that nice of her?' — whether from Bill or someone else — is the understatement that covers the visible devastation of his hopes
jokes.explanation: A cringe-beat that's simultaneously funny and a little heartbreaking — the hallmark of Freaks and Geeks at its best. The understatement of 'Wasn't that nice of her?' does the comedic work.

jokes.text: Lindsay asks Millie about her cousin Toby. Millie: 'No way. Not since he went to jail. He got caught stealing hood ornaments off of cars. They weren't gonna lock him up, but he called the policeman a bad word.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is fishing for a contact who makes fake I.D.s; Millie brings up her cousin Toby
jokes.punchline: Toby's actual incarceration wasn't for the hood ornaments — it was for calling a cop 'a bad word.' The escalation from minor crime to self-inflicted imprisonment is perfect
jokes.explanation: Classic Freaks and Geeks dark-funny storytelling — the real crime that got him locked up being the cop insult rather than the hood ornament theft is a great beat. Millie delivering this with matter-of-fact sadness is character-specific.

jokes.text: Millie: 'He was dropped on the head when he was a baby. That's why we think he's a bad seed.'
jokes.setup: Millie is continuing her family biography of Toby
jokes.punchline: The head-drop causation theory for Toby's criminal lifestyle — delivered with Millie's characteristic earnest conviction
jokes.explanation: Reliable character comedy — Millie's family-explanation mode is funny but this particular beat is a bit familiar.

jokes.text: Millie's verdict on Lindsay's fake I.D. quest: 'That's why there's records.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says she just wants to hear the band play live
jokes.punchline: 'That's why there's records' — Millie's completely literal, practical solution that entirely misses the social point of going to a bar
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect character comedy economy. 'That's why there's records' is a perfectly Millie line — practical, literal, completely right within her frame of reference, completely wrong within Lindsay's. The gap between Millie's world and Lindsay's is the whole joke, delivered in four words. High quotability.

jokes.text: Lindsay mentions she heard the drinking age is 'something like 10 in Idaho.' Millie: 'Well, I don't think that's right.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is grasping at any possible legal way to drink
jokes.punchline: Millie's gentle correction — 'I don't think that's right' — to a claim that is spectacularly wrong is funny in its understatement
jokes.explanation: Good exchange — Millie's polite 'I don't think that's right' to a claim that Idaho has a drinking age of 10 is perfectly understated. The joke is in the mismatch between the absurdity of Lindsay's claim and the mildness of Millie's correction.

jokes.text: The group arrives at Toby's apartment. He answers: 'Look, the pharmacy's closed, all right? I don't got nothin'. I got no stuff. Nothin'.' — He thinks they're there to buy drugs.
jokes.setup: They've come to Toby's for fake I.D.s
jokes.punchline: Toby immediately assumes they're buying drugs and launches into a prepared denial — revealing his primary business is not I.D.s
jokes.explanation: The misdirection establishes Toby's character instantly and efficiently — the 'pharmacy is closed' denial is funnier for being a prepared script. Everything about Toby is suggested by this opening.

jokes.text: 'Only one person is allowed in at a time. One person. You, McMurphy, you come in. You two stay.' [To Nick:] 'Hey, babies. Hey. Hey there. Don't play with that rooster! That's not a nice, petty-pet rooster.'
jokes.setup: Toby establishes his rules for entry — and there is apparently a rooster
jokes.punchline: The rooster is both unexpected and the kind of detail that only exists in certain kinds of apartments. 'Petty-pet rooster' as a category of rooster ('not a nice, petty-pet rooster') implies there is such a thing — just not this one
jokes.explanation: 'Not a nice, petty-pet rooster' is the standout detail — Toby has a threatening rooster in his apartment, and he has a taxonomy of rooster types. The rooster as background worldbuilding for a sketchy ID dealer's apartment is excellent absurdist character comedy.

jokes.text: 'Don't play with that rooster! Don't play with that rooster. That's not a nice, petty-pet rooster. I'm sorry. No, you're the one who'll be sorry.'
jokes.setup: Someone (presumably Daniel) has attempted to interact with a rooster in Toby's apartment
jokes.punchline: Toby's 'petty-pet rooster' phrase — and the promise that you'll be sorry for petting it — suggests the rooster is both terrifying and somehow worse than it looks
jokes.explanation: 'Petty-pet rooster' is a brilliantly weird phrase — Toby clearly made up this category of rooster on the spot, and the specificity of his warning suggests a history of regrettable rooster encounters. The world-building this implies (Toby has a dangerous rooster, has warned people before, has a system for categorizing roosters) is very funny. High originality.

jokes.text: Daniel notices the suspicious number of stereos in Toby's apartment and asks about them. Toby: 'Hey, don't bother with that. Get away from there. Don't even look at that stuff.'
jokes.setup: Toby's apartment is full of obviously stolen electronics
jokes.punchline: Toby's panicked 'don't even look at that stuff' — rather than a normal explanation — confirms immediately that the electronics are stolen
jokes.explanation: Standard 'the denial confirms the crime' beat. Works but is familiar territory.

jokes.text: 'Hey, man, you got a lot of stereos. Where'd you get all this?' / Toby: 'Hey, don't bother with that. Get away from there.'
jokes.setup: Nick is looking around Toby's apartment
jokes.punchline: The obvious implication is the stereos are stolen — Toby's panicked 'don't even look at that stuff' is the confirmation
jokes.explanation: Functional but predictable — the 'don't look at the obviously stolen goods' beat is well-worn. The comedy comes mostly from Toby's escalating alarm, not from the setup itself.

jokes.text: Daniel finds what are clearly drugs growing. Toby: 'I got a green thumb. Don't worry about it.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has found the drug plants
jokes.punchline: 'I got a green thumb' — Toby's horticultural redirect is an absurd understatement for what is presumably a marijuana grow operation
jokes.explanation: Economy is the strength — 'I got a green thumb' is Toby's most concise denial yet. The character consistency (he never admits anything) is funny.

jokes.text: Toby explains why he can't just buy a laminating machine: 'You can't just go into a store and buy one like it's a toaster. Only corporations have that stuff. It's very expensive.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is casually asking about the fake I.D. production process
jokes.punchline: Toby's wounded pride about laminating machine exclusivity — the idea that only corporations have them is both paranoid and probably wrong — reveals he considers his operation sophisticated
jokes.explanation: Toby's professional pride in his fake I.D. apparatus is charming and funny. 'Like it's a toaster' is a good anchor comparison. His conviction that only corporations can afford laminators is Toby in miniature.

jokes.text: Toby directing Daniel for the photo: 'Look like you've been waiting on line for two hours. Look like you don't want to have your picture taken.'
jokes.setup: Toby is directing the I.D. photo session with the authority of a professional photographer
jokes.punchline: The specific direction — 'been waiting two hours,' 'don't want your picture taken' — is a very specific vision of what makes a convincing license photo
jokes.explanation: Toby's expertise is the joke — and his direction is, actually, probably correct. The comedy is that he's thought about this deeply and has a specific aesthetic for authentic misery.

jokes.text: Harris reveals the plan to compete with Vicki's party: 'There's only one thing that could compete with the Vicki Appleby party. All-U-Can-Eat night at the Iron Horse Restaurant.'
jokes.setup: Harris is asked what could possibly compete with a popular-kids' party for Maureen's attention
jokes.punchline: All-U-Can-Eat night — delivered with total confidence as though this is self-evidently superior to a cool party
jokes.explanation: Harris's certainty is the joke — 'Of course. So obvious. To me it is.' The geeks' world where All-U-Can-Eat restaurants are date venues is specific and funny. Sam's 'Of course. So obvious.' response is the right button.

jokes.text: Toby to Lindsay (who he's meeting for the first time): 'My God, you — you are a beautiful girl. A woman. Thanks. When we were kids, you were really cute, but I never thought you would turn out this hot.'
jokes.setup: Toby is meeting Lindsay for the I.D. photos; he appears to know her from childhood
jokes.punchline: His complete inability to observe normal social restraint — saying 'I never thought you would turn out this hot' — is cringe comedy from someone who has clearly been outside normal social interaction too long
jokes.explanation: Toby's social obliviousness is extended throughout his scenes — this is another expression of it. The 'A woman' self-correction is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Toby to Lindsay/Kim: 'My God, you— you are a beautiful girl. A woman... When we were kids, you were really cute, but, I mean, I never thought you would turn out this hot.'
jokes.setup: Toby recognizes Kim/Lindsay as Millie's family connection
jokes.punchline: Toby hitting on the teenager he's providing fake IDs to — while noting he knew her 'when we were kids' — is peak inappropriate oversharing from a character already established as having no social calibration
jokes.explanation: The cringe is functional — Toby's complete social miscalibration is established, and hitting on a minor he's helping commit fraud while reminiscing about knowing her as a child is effectively uncomfortable. Less original than Toby's laminating machine bit but consistent character work.

jokes.text: Toby casually mentions about McKinley: 'It's a pretty good school. I went there. I almost graduated from there a few years ago.'
jokes.setup: Toby is making conversation about the school
jokes.punchline: 'I almost graduated' — presented as a perfectly normal credential, as though near-graduation is an achievement worth citing
jokes.explanation: 'I almost graduated' as a point of pride is perfect Toby characterization — he's entirely sincere about his near-achievement. Economy is excellent: two words do the whole character job.

jokes.text: Toby invites Lindsay to see Yes at Cobo with 'Richie Blackmore's Rainbow opening up' while actively producing her fake I.D. — and Nick has to announce she's his girlfriend to stop it.
jokes.setup: Toby is photographing Lindsay for her fake I.D. while hitting on her
jokes.punchline: The simultaneous criminal enterprise and romantic pursuit — and Toby's complete failure to find this contradictory — is the joke
jokes.explanation: The cringe comedy of being hit on during an illegal transaction is well-observed. Toby multitasking these activities with no awareness of the contradiction is his defining quality.

jokes.text: Nick claims Lindsay is his girlfriend to stop Toby's flirtation. Lindsay backs him up. Toby: 'Hey, man, that's not cool. You know, now you make me feel like a fool for asking for your number.' Lindsay: 'No, you can have my phone number. It's just that I have a boyfriend.'
jokes.setup: Toby is embarrassed by the revelation that Lindsay has a boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Toby's wounded dignity — feeling foolish for asking — and Lindsay's kind clarification that he CAN have the number (just contextually) is a strange inversion of normal social logic
jokes.explanation: Toby's social code — being polite enough to feel shamed but not polite enough to have not asked — is internally consistent and character-specific.

jokes.text: The price negotiation: 'It's 75 each.' 'Hey, I thought you said it was 40 each.' 'Did I?' 'I think so.' '100 each.'
jokes.setup: The price is being agreed on at the end of the fake I.D. session
jokes.punchline: Toby's correction to 40 triggers an immediate counter-correction to 100 — his price negotiation strategy is to increase the price when challenged
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical — the 'Did I?' followed by doubling the price when pressed is a masterful con-man move. The structure is crisp and the escalation is the joke. Very funny exchange.

jokes.text: Toby looks at Lindsay's real license photo: 'Look at how much hotter you look in this one than in your real driver's license.' Nick: 'Whoa, is this one fake, too?' Lindsay: 'No, why?' Nick: 'Because it says you're 18.'
jokes.setup: Toby is comparing the fake I.D. photo to Lindsay's real license
jokes.punchline: Nick's innocent question reveals he didn't know Lindsay's age, which leads to the real reveal
jokes.explanation: Good multi-beat joke: Toby's backhanded compliment, Nick's confusion, and the real reveal. The setup is Toby's observation; the punchline is Nick genuinely not knowing Lindsay's age, which sets up the next joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'So. Was left back one year.' Nick: 'Well, I know. But still, it wouldn't make you 18.' Lindsay: 'All right, I was left back twice. OK? Are you happy now? There's no secrets between us. All right. 18, that makes me jail bait.'
jokes.setup: Nick has deduced Lindsay shouldn't be 18
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's escalating admissions — left back once, left back TWICE — building to 'there's no secrets between us' and then the entirely inappropriate 'that makes me jail bait' directed at her sort-of-boyfriend
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation structure. The two-step confession (once, then twice) builds well, and 'there's no secrets between us' — as though she's clearing the air for a relationship — followed by 'that makes me jail bait' is perfectly timed. The 'jail bait' observation directed at Nick is the kicker. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: Toby immediately panics: 'Hey! You better watch out. I'm gonna call the cops on you. Officer, I didn't know. Look at her, she looks like she's 30.' Lindsay: 'Ahhh! No I don't!'
jokes.setup: Toby has learned the girl he was hitting on is under-age
jokes.punchline: Toby's immediate self-protective panic — threatening to call the cops on himself for his own illegal transaction — and his defense that 'she looks 30' is both an insult and an alibi
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Toby's instant self-righteous panic — threatening to call the cops mid-illegal transaction, while insulting Lindsay by claiming she looks 30 to justify his flirting — is character comedy at its most precise. Lindsay's wounded 'No I don't!' is the perfect button. The irony of a criminal threatening to call the police on himself is delicious.

jokes.text: Harold and Jean play Pit with just two people. Harold wins a corner on barley. Jean: 'What happened to the corn?' Harold: 'I'm looking for corn, here.'
jokes.setup: Harold and Jean are playing Pit alone, the children having abandoned them
jokes.punchline: The visual comedy of two adults playing a yelling stock-trading game alone, competing against each other, is the joke — plus the dialogue reveals the utter inadequacy of two-person Pit
jokes.explanation: The Pit callback pays off beautifully — Harold's 'enjoying it damn it' has led to the sad image of two people playing a market-trading game against each other. The mundanity of 'What happened to the corn?' as their conversation is the correct note.

jokes.text: After the kids leave, Harold admits: 'This is a terrible game with just two people.' Jean's response: 'Nobody's home... You wanna have a little sex?'
jokes.setup: Harold and Jean are alone; Harold has conceded Pit is bad with two people
jokes.punchline: Jean's pivot — the empty house means sex, not continued Pit — and the phrasing 'a little sex' (not particularly exciting, very marital) is both funny and sweet
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. 'A little sex' is the perfect phrasing — not 'do you want to make love' or anything romantic, just the pragmatic marital offer of 'a little sex' when the house is empty. It's funny and also a loving portrait of long-married people. Harold's 'Sex?' reaction and then 'Let's go' is the button. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: The geeks explain all-you-can-eat strategy to Maureen: 'We order a lot of food, and then keep asking for more until they go out of business.' 'My mom says it takes a while for your stomach to tell your brain it's full, so you should eat really fast.' 'They take forever on purpose so you'll get full while you wait.'
jokes.setup: Maureen asks how all-you-can-eat works
jokes.punchline: The boys have a comprehensive, tested strategy for defeating all-you-can-eat restaurants — with specific insights about the waiter slowness conspiracy — revealing how often they've thought about this
jokes.explanation: The geeks' all-you-can-eat philosophy is both observational comedy and character work — they have deeply considered this. The waiter conspiracy theory ('they take forever on purpose') is the best detail.

jokes.text: Sam directly tells the waiter: 'So, I hear the service here is really bad 'cause you don't want us to eat much.' Waiter: 'I don't own the place. I don't care how much you eat.' Sam: 'Are you sure?' Waiter: 'No, actually, I'm a millionaire and I just do this for fun.'
jokes.setup: Sam has been warned about the restaurant conspiracy and immediately challenges the waiter with it
jokes.punchline: The waiter's 'I'm a millionaire and I just do this for fun' — a perfect dry deadpan that both mocks Sam's paranoia and suggests a lifetime of dealing with difficult customers
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the conspiracy setup. The waiter's 'I'm a millionaire and I just do this for fun' is a perfect deadpan retort — deeply sarcastic, immediately deflating, and delivered with the weariness of someone who's heard a lot of nonsense. The callback makes it richer. One of the cleaner joke structures in the episode.

jokes.text: After Sam's confrontation, the waiter offers a deal: '20% tip, and I'll bring 'em faster than you can eat 'em.' Neal: '20? I thought 10 was the norm.' Waiter: '15 is standard. 20 is classy. Why are we still talking? I want some ribs.'
jokes.setup: The waiter offers a tip-for-speed deal
jokes.punchline: 'Why are we still talking? I want some ribs' — the waiter's impatience mirrors the geeks' own enthusiasm and suggests he's actually invested in this now
jokes.explanation: '15 is standard. 20 is classy' is a good waiter-coda — he's got a whole taxonomy of tipping worked out. 'I want some ribs' as his motivator for efficient service is a funny character flip.

jokes.text: The waiter brings the bread and forgets it: 'Oh, I forgot your bread.' The geeks all react to Sam's prediction coming true.
jokes.setup: Sam predicted the restaurant would withhold bread; the waiter proves him right by forgetting it
jokes.punchline: The visual reaction beat — the geeks' vindicated looks when the bread arrives late — rewards the earlier conspiracy theory
jokes.explanation: A competent callback that validates the earlier bit. The comedy is in the geeks' reaction more than the line itself.

tmdb_overview: Lindsay reluctantly agrees to come over to Nick's house while his parents are out of town. Sam hopes to become lab partners with Cindy, but becomes jealous when Bill is named Cindy's lab partner.
